paper_title: Time-Series Foundation Model Embeddings for Remaining Useful Life Estimation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Remaining Useful Life (RUL) prediction is essential for industrial predictive maintenance, yet many learning-based approaches rely on extensive feature engineering or large labeled datasets to train task-specific sequence models. In this work, we introduce a lightweight learning approach, in which we leverage a frozen pretrained time-series foundation model (TSFM) and combine it with a small regression head for RUL estimation from multivariate sensor streams. More specifically, we use Chronos-2 as a frozen backbone to extract context window features and train a lightweight regression neural network for RUL prediction. Experiments on real-world industrial sensor data from two device types show that Chronos-2 features consistently improve over recurrent, convolutional, Transformer-based, and gradient-boosting baselines under the same preprocessing and evaluation protocol. We further analyze the impact of context length and find that performance improves significantly with longer histories, indicating that TSFM representation offer a practical and data-efficient alternative for RUL estimation in industrial settings.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Time-Series Foundation Model Embeddings for Remaining Useful Life Estimation Amir El-Ghoussani Michele De Vita Ronald Naumann Vasileios Belagiannis Friedrich-Alexander-Universitat ErlangenNurnberg (FAU), Germany {amir.el-ghoussani, michele.de.vita, vasileios.belagiannis}@fau.de Nokia Solutions and Networks GmbH & Co. KG, Germany ronald.naumann@nokia.com 6 2 0 2 0 1 ] . [ 1 0 9 9 1 1 . 6 0 6 2 : r AbstractRemaining Useful Life (RUL) prediction is essential for industrial predictive maintenance, yet many learning-based approaches rely on extensive feature engineering or large labeled datasets to train task-specific sequence models. In this work, we introduce lightweight learning approach, in which we leverage frozen pretrained time-series foundation model (TSFM) and combine it with small regression head for RUL estimation from multivariate sensor streams. More specifically, we use Chronos2 as frozen backbone to extract context window features and train lightweight regression neural network for RUL prediction. Experiments on real-world industrial sensor data from two device types show that Chronos-2 features consistently improve over recurrent, convolutional, Transformer-based, and gradientboosting baselines under the same preprocessing and evaluation protocol. We further analyze the impact of context length and find that performance improves significantly with longer histories, indicating that TSFM representation offer practical and dataefficient alternative for RUL estimation in industrial settings. Index Termsremaining useful life, predictive maintenance, time series, in-context learning, foundation models, Chronos 2 I. INTRODUCTION Predictive maintenance aims to forecast equipment failures, so that interventions can be planned in advance to minimise unexpected downtime and associated costs. key metric in this context is the Remaining Useful Life (RUL) that is defined as the estimated time until an assets failure based on its current condition. Predicting device RUL based on multivariate sensor streams accurately is vital for effective maintenance, and is therefore major focus in the prognostics field [1], [2]. Traditional data-driven RUL estimation usually relies on either handcrafted features or end-to-end sequence models, which have been trained using multivariate historical sensor measurements [3]. For one-dimensional sensor signals, encoder-decoder denoising with adversarial latent alignment have been used to learn noise-robust representations [4]. While deeper sequence models such as long short-term memory (LSTM) networks [5] and gated recurrent units (GRU) [6] can capture temporal patterns, they often require substantial labeled data and careful tuning for different sensor types. To address these limitations, temporal convolutional neural networks (CNNs) [7] and Transformer-based [8] models have been proposed for representation learning and long-range dependencies. Transformer-style architectures with hierarchical or multiscale attention are increasingly used to model sensor interactions over time in multivariate signal streams [9]. To

sections.title: We subdivide the multivariate sensor
sections.content: stream into context windows of length L.

sections.title: We feed each window into a frozen
sections.content: pretrained TSFM to extract temporal context features.

sections.title: We train a lightweight regression
sections.content: head with embeddings Ht features to predict RUL. Fig. 1. Overview of our lightweight approach to RUL estimation on industrial data. relax the labeling requirements, contrastive and self-supervised representation learning has also been used to pretrain on unlabeled sensor data before supervised RUL regression [10]. Related label-efficient temporal monitoring problems have also been studied in unsupervised anomaly detection, where models learn normal multi-agent trajectory behavior from unlabeled data and are evaluated on annotated abnormal scenarios [11]. Robustness to distribution shift under varying operating conditions is often tackled via adversarial domain adaptation [12]. In parallel, physics-informed hybrids introduce degradation structure to improve extrapolation and interpretability [13]. Generative diffusion approaches are explored for data augmentation and uncertainty modeling, both via diffusion models for generating realistic degradation trajectories [14] and stochastic diffusion-process models that predict RUL distributions [15]. However, these approaches require training on task-specific data, which can be resource-intensive and may not generalize well across different datasets. In contrast, we find that timeseries foundation models (TSFMs) provide way to obtain temporally rich representations without the need for extensive training. TSFMs are predominantly trained and used for time-series forecasting tasks. Recent approaches such as Chronos [8] and its successor CHRONOS-2 [16] are probabilistic transformerbased TSFMs. Both versions are built on decoder-only transformer architecture that tokenizes input time series into discrete bins. CHRONOS-2 enables efficient in-context learning without requiring fine-tuning. Kairos [17] uses masked token modeling to learn representations for multivariate time series. VisionTS++ [18] applies vision transformer architectures to time-series patches for improved long-range modeling. TimesFM [19] leverages large-scale pretraining with masked forecasting objective. PatchTST [20] divides time series into patches and processes them with transformers for efficient sequence modeling. MiroAI [21], is based on masked autoencoding and large-scale pretraining. In this work, we introduce an approach leveraging pretrained TSFM representations for RUL estimation from large-scale sensor streams. We extend TSFMs beyond their traditional forecasting use case by treating RUL prediction as supervised regression problem. We adapt CHRONOS-2 by loading lookback window of past sensor measurements directly into the models context. From this context, we extract backbone representations and train lightweight regression head on top, keeping the adaptation simple and efficient. Experiments on large-scale industrial sensor dataset show that conditioning CHRONOS-2 on sensor-history context yields substantial gains, outperforming standard baselinesincluding non-sequential regressorsby up to 5. We further find that context length is critical: expanding the context window to 80 steps dramatically improves performance by reducing MAE by 2 compared to short 5-step context. II. METHOD To predict the RUL, we process the complete history of multivariate sensor data. Let RT represent the entire sequence of sensor measurements: = [x1, . . . , xT ], (1) where each xt is D-dimensional vector of sensor readings at time t, and is the total length of the time series. The goal is to estimate the RUL, denoted as yt, for each time step t. We approach this by mapping the input sequence to sequence of RUL estimates via combination of 2 models, consisting of frozen pre-trained backbone Φ() and trainable regression head gϕ(): ˆyt = gϕ(Φ(X)t). (2) The following subsections detail the data processing pipeline, the specific architecture of the backbone and head, and our training protocol. A. Data Preprocessing and Label Generation Raw sensor measurements are recorded at irregular timestamps due to network delays and asynchronous hardware. However, most pretrained TSFMs, including CHRONOS, require regularly sampled data. For this reason, we linearly interpolate each sensor record to uniform time grid with step size t. Given irregular timestamps τk and τk+1, we linearly interpolate the value xt at regular time (where τk τk+1) as: xt = xτk + (xτk+1 xτk ) . (3) τk τk+1 τk This yields multivariate sequence with regular intervals {xt}T t=1. To prevent artifacts from long outages, we discard intervals where the gap between consecutive raw samples is higher than threshold tmax (τk+1 τk > tmax) and only interpolate within valid ranges. Following resampling, we filter out faulty measurements containing NaN values. We apply global outlier clipping strategy, clamping values to the 1st and 99th percentiles, and normalize sensor channels to zero mean and unit variance. All statistics are computed exclusively on the training split to prevent data leakage. a) RUL Label Construction: In addition to sensor measurements, we utilize maintenance logs containing the set of failure timestamps Trep. For given timestamp t, the time of the next failure is defined as: tfail = min{t Trep : > t}. (4) The RUL label is computed as the time remaining until this event: yt = tfail t. (5) We express yt in days and cap such that yt min(yt, ymax), with ymax = 1000 days. Timestamps with no subsequent repair events are excluded from the supervised training set. B. Model Architecture Our approach leverages CHRONOS-2, pretrained TSFM designed for probabilistic forecasting, as fixed feature extractor. We specifically utilize the chronos2 checkpoint. The TSFM follows decoder-only transformer architecture. a) Context Extraction: We utilize the CHRONOS2model, denoted as Φe, to extract high-dimensional features from windowed sensor sequence XtL:t. By processing the sequence up to in window of timesteps, we make use of CHRONOS-2s in-context learning capabilities, i.e. we extract embeddings that capture temporal context and dependencies across the context window of size L. Keeping the TSFM parameters frozen, we obtain sequence of hidden representations: = Φe(XtL:t) RLh, (6) where is the hidden dimension of the model and = [htL, . . . , ht]. b) RUL Estimation: To predict the RUL at specific time step t, we use the corresponding embedding vector htL:t from the sequence HtL:t over the context window L. We employ regressor head gϕ with parameters ϕ: ˆyt = gϕ(htL:t). (7) The head is implemented as Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) consisting of two linear layers with hidden width of m, utilizing ReLU activation. final ReLU is applied to the output to enforce ˆyt 0. We apply dropout with rate after the first hidden layer and optimize only the head parameters ϕ. III. EXPERIMENTS A. Dataset and experimental setup Our main experiments are conducted on industrial sensor datasets provided by Nokia Solutions and Networks GmbH & Co. KG, Germany, where the failure events are provided in repair logs. The datasets contain multivariate sensor streams from two different devices; we refer to them as Device and Device B. We preprocess the data by resampling the sensor streams to uniform time grid, normalizing each sensor channel, and computing RUL labels from the repair logs as described in Sec. II-A. For Device A, after preprocessing we obtain total of 297,345 labeled samples. The sensors return multivariate sensor recordings with = 87, and we set the resampling step size to = 1 hour. The context window length is set to 5 in the following experiments. In addition, we evaluate on second dataset from Device B. After the same preprocessing procedure, Device contains 119,364 annotated samples with = 51 sensor channels. We use the same resampling step size and context length L. To prevent temporal leakage, we employ chronological split. The training set consists of windows ending at Ttrain, while the test set contains windows where > Ttest. We select split points to achieve an 85:15 ratio. Crucially, we discard any window [t, ..., ] that overlaps the boundary between splits. We report performance on the held-out test set using Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Mean Squared Error (MSE). B. Implementation We train our approach using the Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss and the Adam optimizer [22] with learning rate of 103. Training runs for maximum of 50 epochs with batch size of 64. The fine-tuning process requires about 2 hours on single NVIDIA A6000 GPU. We train separate models for device ( 300K parameters) and device ( 250K parameters). C. Baselines We compare our approach against classical, non-sequential regressors trained on raw datapoints and lightweight sequential neural baselines trained on sequences with context windows length L. For the non-sequential setting, we evaluate linear regression and random forests [23] on normalized sensor vectors xt, using the same capped target yt and the same train/test splits as our method. For sequential baselines, we train LSTM [5] and GRU [24] regressors on the windowed inputs XtL:t RLD and predict ˆyt from the final recurrent state via linear readout. We additionally train gradient boosting [25] on window features ut = φ(XtL:t), where φ() computes per-channel statistical features (mean, std, min, max, quantiles), last value, TABLE SEQUENCE MODELS ON 5-STEP WINDOWS FOR DEVICE AND DEVICE B. Device Device Method LinReg RandForest GrBoost GRU LSTM TCN / Temporal CNN Transformer (encoder) Ours MAE MSE MAE MSE 22123 15655 33400 9523 10212 11435 8900 7212 44123 48655 53400 10236 12400 9689 10500 6513 173 165 186 91 93 88 90 44 120 112 130 102 99 112 95 64 first differences, and linear trend slope over the time steps. This baseline uses the same L, preprocessing pipeline, and chronological splits as our method. We further evaluate two strong convolutional and attentionbased sequence models: Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) [26] and Transformer encoder [27] regressor. Both models take XtL:t as input and output scalar RUL prediction through pooling operation over the time dimension followed by linear output layer. All baseline hyperparameters are tuned on validation split of 10% of training data and evaluated using the same metrics as our method. Preprocessing statistics, such as normalization and clipping thresholds are computed on the training split only and applied unchanged to the test splits. D. Performance on 5-step windows In Table I, we report results for sequence models trained on = 5-step windows for both Device and Device B. On both datasets, moving from non-neural approaches (upper block) to neural network-based sequence modeling (lower block) yields substantial improvement, indicating that even short contextwindows carry significant information. Our CHRONOS-2 adaptation further improves performance across both devices: conditioning CHRONOS-2 on temporal context, extracting internal embeddings, and adding dedicated regression head achieves the best overall performance among evaluated models. E. Effect of Context Length We study how the length of context windows affects CHRONOS-2 on both devices by varying L. For each L, we generate the windowed inputs XtL:t using the same resampling rate and the same chronological split protocol, keep the CHRONOS-2 backbone frozen, and retrain only the MLP head. We leave all other hyperparameters fixed. Each setting is evaluated after training our approach for 50 epochs. For the remaining baselines, we retrain and re-tune for each using the same validation split. Figure 2 shows the effect of varying context length for Device A. The performance of our approach improves significantly as increases, with the most substantial gains observed up to = 80. Beyond this point, performance saturates. In contrast, other baselines show only mild fluctuations with increasing L, indicating that they do not effectively leverage longer context windows. The TCN and Transformer baselines Device IV. CONCLUSION 90 80 70 60 50 40 30 20 Ours TCN Transformer GRU LSTM GradBoost 0 20 40 60 80 Context length Fig. 2. MAE versus context length on Device A. Baselines show mild fluctuations; TCN and Transformer are the strongest baselines while Ours shows significantimprovement over all basselines. TABLE II ABLATION ON REGRESSION HEAD ARCHITECTURE. Head TCN (for comparison) Linear 2-layer MLP 4-layer MLP MAE MSE 9689 8044 6513 6342 88 60 44 are the strongest among the non-TSFM approaches, but still significantly underperform compared to our method across all context lengths. F. Ablation: Regression head We ablate the capacity of the supervised regression head while keeping the Chronos-2 backbone frozen in table II. Specifically, we replace our default MLP head with linear head and deeper MLP variant (4 layers), using identical preprocessing, context length and training budget. All ablations are done on device A.

sections.title: Our goal
sections.content: the perforis to determine how much of mance gain can be attributed to the pretrained CHRONOS2representations versus the supervised regression head. If CHRONOS-2representations are the primary factor of performance, then simple linear head should already achieve most of the performance improvemnt, and increasing head capacity should provide only small improvements. On theother hand, if performance improves as the head becomes deeper, this indicates that large fraction of the mapping from inputs XtL:t to RUL yt is learned by the regression head rather than the pretrained backbone. When compared to the TCN baseline, even linear head on top of frozen CHRONOS-2embeddings achieves significant improvement (MAE 60 vs. 88). This shows that the pretrained backbone provides strong representation for RUL estimation. The 2-layer MLP outperforms the linear head, indicating that some nonlinearity is beneficial for mapping CHRONOS2embeddings to RUL predictions. However, increasing the head capacity to 4 layers does not yield significant further improvements. This paper studied whether pretrained time-series foundation model can serve as an effective feature extractor for RUL estimation in industrial predictive maintenance. In our approach, simple adaptation that leverages the frozen CHRONOS-2backbone , we extract context-dependent embeddings from multivariate sensor windows, and train lightweight regressor to predict RUL. On the Nokia dataset, the approach achieved lower error than broad set of classical and deep learning baselines, and an ablation over context length showed that longer histories can substantially improve performance. Beyond raw performance, we discussed evaluation and modeling choices that are critical for trustworthy RUL results. As future work, we plan to (i) leverage Chronos-2s probabilistic outputs to provide calibrated uncertainty for maintenance decisions, (ii) improve multivariate tokenization and channel handling for heterogeneous sensors, and (iii) evaluate robustness under missing sensors, operating-regime shifts, and lowlabel settings.
